summary: SpaceX supply/demand dynamics are favorable post-IPO due to employee ownership concentration and prior liquidity opportunities reducing overhang
summary_extended: Baker argues that SpaceX's post-IPO supply/demand dynamics will be unusually favorable compared to typical IPO lockup situations. His core reasoning is that over 10,000 SpaceX employees purchased stock on the IPO itself, and that employees and long-term investors have had liquidity via tender offers every six months for approximately ten years — meaning anyone who wanted to sell has already had ample opportunity to do so. This dramatically reduces the post-IPO supply overhang that typically plagues newly public companies when lockup periods expire, as the usual pent-up seller base has already been absorbed. The investment implication is that SpaceX shares should face less selling pressure post-IPO than the market might expect, making the stock a potentially attractive buy around the IPO period. Baker presents this as a contrarian structural view on IPO mechanics rather than a fundamental business thesis.
detail: 10,000+ SpaceX employees bought stock on the IPO. Employees and long-term investors have had liquidity every 6 months for 10 years via tender offers — those who wanted to sell already did. This reduces post-IPO supply overhang significantly vs. typical IPO lockup dynamics.
confidence_evidence: Baker presents specific data point (10K+ employees bought on IPO) and logical framework for reduced supply overhang
quote_fragment: 10,000 SpaceX employees bought on the IPO

ticker_audit.reasoning: SpaceX is not yet publicly listed; Virgin Galactic (SPCE) is a separate company and is not the subject of this thesis. No US-listed equity directly maps to SpaceX at this time.
